skills.name: Abduction | Peirce: inference generating explanatory hypotheses (distinct from IBE evaluation)
skills.description: Abduction in Peirce's sense is the inferential stage that responds to surprising facts by generating explanatory hypotheses for further inquiry. It is distinct from later inference-to-the-best-explanation evaluation: abduction proposes a plausible explanation, deduction draws its consequences, and induction tests it.

skills.name: Advaita Vedanta
skills.description: Advaita Vedanta (Sanskrit: 'non-dual end of the Vedas') is the dominant school of Hindu philosophical theology, systematized by Adi Shankaracharya (8th century CE), holding that ultimate reality is a single, undivided consciousness (Brahman), that the individual self (Atman) is identical with Brahman, and that the apparent multiplicity of the world is a superimposition (maya/adhyasa) on this non-dual ground.

skills.name: Ahimsa
skills.description: Ahimsa (Sanskrit: 'non-harm' or 'non-violence') is the foundational ethical principle across Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain traditions holding that causing injury—physical, mental, or structural—to any sentient being is the primary ethical wrong. In its active form, it demands not merely refraining from violence but the cultivation of compassion and the structural removal of harm.

skills.name: Anekantavada
skills.description: Anekantavada (Sanskrit: anekānta-vāda, 'doctrine of many-sidedness') is the Jain epistemological and metaphysical doctrine that reality is complex, multifaceted, and cannot be fully captured by any single perspective. Paired with Syadvada (the doctrine of conditional predication) and Nayavada (the doctrine of partial standpoints), it holds that any claim about reality is true only from a particular viewpoint (naya), and that intellectual humility, conditionality in assertion, and openness to other perspectives are both epistemically and ethically required.

skills.name: Antifragility
skills.description: Antifragility is Nassim Nicholas Taleb's framework for identifying and designing systems that benefit from volatility, stressors, errors, variation, and time because their payoff to disorder is convex rather than merely robust.

skills.name: Argumentation Theory | Toulmin model: claim, grounds, warrant, backing, rebuttal
skills.description: Argumentation Theory in Toulmin's model analyzes practical arguments by mapping claims, grounds, warrants, backing, qualifiers, and rebuttals, emphasizing field-dependent standards of support rather than treating all reasoning as formal deduction.

skills.name: Aristotelian Rhetoric
skills.description: Aristotelian Rhetoric is the art of discovering the available means of persuasion in civic situations, using ethos, pathos, logos, enthymeme, genre, and audience adaptation to reason about matters that cannot be settled by demonstration alone.

skills.name: Aristotelian Virtue Ethics
skills.description: Aristotelian Virtue Ethics holds that morality is fundamentally about the development of excellent character traits (virtues) that constitute and enable human flourishing (eudaimonia). Rather than asking 'what should I do?', it asks 'what kind of person should I be?' — grounding moral action in practical wisdom and the cultivation of character over time.

skills.name: Auction Theory
skills.description: Auction Theory studies how auction formats, bidder information, valuation structures, and strategic incentives determine bids, allocation efficiency, seller revenue, and risks such as the winner's curse.

skills.name: Augustinian Theology
skills.description: Augustinian Theology is the systematic theological vision of Augustine of Hippo (354–430 CE), centering on the radical dependence of all creation on God, the corruption of human nature by original sin, the sovereignty of divine grace, and the pilgrimage of the soul (and the City of God) through history toward its rest in God. It provides a framework for analyzing human motivation, social life, history, and suffering through the lens of disordered love (cupiditas) versus ordered love (caritas).

skills.name: Bayesian Epistemology
skills.description: Bayesian Epistemology is the view that rational degrees of belief (credences) obey the probability calculus, and that rational belief revision consists in updating credences by conditionalization on new evidence using Bayes's theorem. It provides a formal framework for modeling evidence, confirmation, and rational inference under uncertainty.

skills.name: Bounded Rationality
skills.description: Bounded Rationality is Herbert Simon's framework for decision-making under limits of cognition, time, information, and computation, explaining how people and organizations use search, routines, and heuristics instead of full optimization.

skills.name: Capabilities Approach | Sen/Nussbaum: freedom as real opportunity to function
skills.description: Capabilities Approach | Sen/Nussbaum: freedom as real opportunity to function evaluates justice and development by the substantive freedoms people actually have to achieve valuable beings and doings, rather than by resources, utility, or formal liberties alone.

skills.name: Care Ethics | Gilligan/Noddings: relationships and dependency as moral center
skills.description: Care Ethics places relationships, dependency, responsiveness, and the moral significance of particular persons at the center of ethical reasoning, challenging moral theories that privilege abstract rules, impartial calculation, or autonomous individuals.

skills.name: Causal Inference
skills.description: Causal Inference in Pearl's structural causal model tradition uses directed acyclic graphs, interventions, the do-operator, and counterfactual semantics to distinguish causal effects from association and to determine when causal questions are identifiable from data.

skills.name: Chesterton's Fence
skills.description: Chesterton's Fence is a conservative-reform heuristic: before abolishing a rule, institution, or constraint that seems pointless, understand what function it served and what problem its removal may revive.

skills.name: Classical Liberalism | individual liberty, limited government, spontaneous order
skills.description: Classical Liberalism | individual liberty, limited government, spontaneous order defends individual freedom, private property, rule of law, free exchange, civil society, and constitutional limits on coercive power, while treating many beneficial social orders as emergent rather than centrally designed.

skills.name: Classical Logic
skills.description: Classical Logic analyzes deductive inference through validity, soundness, syllogism, propositional connectives, and predicate quantification under bivalent truth-preserving semantics.

skills.name: Coasean Bargaining | externalities, transaction costs, allocation of rights (Coase theorem)
skills.description: Coasean Bargaining analyzes externalities by assigning rights, estimating transaction costs, and asking whether parties can bargain to a higher-valued allocation; the Coase theorem shows that with zero transaction costs and clear entitlements, bargaining reaches efficient outcomes regardless of initial rights.

skills.name: Cognitive Behavioral Framing | CBT: thoughts-feelings-behavior loops, cognitive distortions
skills.description: Cognitive Behavioral Framing analyzes distress and dysfunction as maintained by reciprocal loops among situations, automatic thoughts, emotions, bodily sensations, and behaviors, with change produced by testing beliefs, modifying behavior, and building alternative interpretations.

skills.name: Coherentism
skills.description: Coherentism is an epistemological theory on which justification arises from the mutual support, consistency, and explanatory integration of beliefs within a whole system, rather than from privileged basic beliefs or an external reliability condition. It answers the regress problem by treating justification as holistic and networked.

skills.name: Communitarianism | MacIntyre/Sandel: selves and goods constituted by community
skills.description: Communitarianism | MacIntyre/Sandel: selves and goods constituted by community criticizes liberal individualism by arguing that persons, obligations, virtues, and public goods are formed within communities, traditions, roles, and shared practices.

skills.name: Comparative Advantage | Ricardo: gains from trade even with absolute inferiority
skills.description: Comparative Advantage, classically associated with David Ricardo, explains how parties can gain from specialization and trade according to relative opportunity costs, even when one party is absolutely less productive in every activity.

skills.name: Conceptual Analysis
skills.description: Conceptual Analysis is the philosophical method of clarifying a concept by identifying the necessary and sufficient conditions for its application — typically by examining what cases clearly fall under the concept, what cases clearly do not, and what intuitions govern borderline cases. It is the dominant method of analytic philosophy, aiming to articulate what we implicitly know when we competently use a concept.

skills.name: Confucian Ren and Li
skills.description: Ren (仁, benevolence/humaneness) and Li (禮, ritual propriety/rites) are the two foundational concepts of Confucian ethics. Ren is the inner virtue of genuine care, empathy, and moral goodness toward others — the character of a person who is truly human; Li is the outer expression of that virtue through proper conduct, ceremony, and the roles and relationships that constitute social life. Together they describe a unified view of the good person as one whose inner character is expressed through, and cultivated by, the proper forms of social conduct.

skills.name: Contractarianism
skills.description: Contractarianism is the moral and political framework that explains norms as the outcome of rational agreement among self-interested agents seeking mutual advantage. In Hobbesian and Gauthierian forms, morality constrains direct maximization because enforceable or credible cooperation makes each participant better off than conflict, predation, or failed bargaining.

skills.name: Contractualism
skills.description: Contractualism, in T. M. Scanlon's sense, is the moral framework on which principles are acceptable only if no person could reasonably reject them as terms for living with others. It evaluates actions by their justifiability to each affected person, using individual complaints against candidate principles rather than aggregating welfare.

skills.name: Control Theory Thinking
skills.description: Control Theory Thinking analyzes systems as feedback loops with setpoints, sensors, error signals, actuators, delays, gains, disturbances, and stability constraints.

skills.name: Counterfactual Reasoning
skills.description: Counterfactual Reasoning is the cognitive and formal practice of evaluating what would have happened (or would happen) if conditions had been (or were) different from how they actually are, enabling analysis of causation, responsibility, decision-making, and policy by comparing the actual world to relevant alternatives. It underlies causal inference, historical analysis, regret-based learning, and the evaluation of interventions.

skills.name: Creative Destruction | Schumpeter: innovation destroying incumbent structures
skills.description: Creative Destruction is Schumpeter's account of capitalist development as waves of entrepreneurial innovation that create new products, methods, markets, and organizations while destroying incumbent structures, assets, and routines.

skills.name: Cybernetics | Wiener/Ashby: requisite variety, homeostasis, communication and control
skills.description: Cybernetics is the study of communication, feedback, and control in animals, machines, organizations, and social systems, associated with Norbert Wiener, W. Ross Ashby, Stafford Beer, and concepts such as homeostasis, circular causality, and requisite variety.

skills.name: Cynefin Framework
skills.description: The Cynefin Framework is Dave Snowden's sense-making framework for matching action to context across clear, complicated, complex, chaotic, and disordered domains. It distinguishes situations by the visibility and stability of cause-effect relationships and prescribes different decision logics for each domain.

skills.name: Decision Theory
skills.description: Decision Theory is the formal study of how rational agents should choose among alternatives under conditions of uncertainty, combining probability assignments over outcomes with utility (preference) values to identify the option that maximizes expected utility. It encompasses both normative theory (how one ought to decide) and descriptive theory (how people actually decide).

skills.name: Deconstruction | Derrida: destabilizing binary oppositions, différance, close reading against the grain
skills.description: Deconstruction, associated with Jacques Derrida, is a practice of close reading that exposes how texts depend on unstable binary oppositions, deferred meaning, and excluded terms that undermine the hierarchies they appear to secure.

skills.name: Defense in Depth
skills.description: Defense in Depth is a safety and security framework that protects critical functions with multiple, independent, diverse, and monitored layers so that no single failure, bypass, or human error can directly produce catastrophe; Reason's Swiss cheese model explains accidents as alignment of holes across layers.

skills.name: Dependent Origination
skills.description: Dependent Origination (Pali: paticca-samuppada; Sanskrit: pratityasamutpada) is the Buddhist doctrine that all phenomena arise in dependence on conditions — nothing exists independently, permanently, or by its own essence. The classical formulation is a twelve-link chain tracing how ignorance gives rise, through a series of conditioned steps, to the full cycle of suffering and rebirth. More broadly, it is a universal causal principle: 'When this exists, that comes to be; with the arising of this, that arises. When this does not exist, that does not come to be; with the cessation of this, that ceases.'

skills.name: Divine Command Theory
skills.description: Divine Command Theory is the metaethical view that moral obligation is grounded in God's commands: what one ought to do is what God commands, and what one must not do is what God forbids. It analyzes morality through divine authority, revelation, obedience, and the Euthyphro dilemma about whether goodness is independent of or constituted by divine command.

skills.name: Double-Loop Learning
skills.description: Double-Loop Learning is Chris Argyris and Donald Schon's framework for distinguishing ordinary error correction within existing governing assumptions from deeper learning that questions and revises those assumptions themselves.

skills.name: Dual-Process Theory | System 1 / System 2 cognition (Kahneman, Stanovich)
skills.description: Dual-Process Theory distinguishes fast, automatic, associative cognition from slower, controlled, reflective cognition, explaining how intuitive judgments can be useful, biased, overridden, or rationalized depending on attention, expertise, and task structure.

skills.name: Effective Altruism
skills.description: Effective Altruism is a practical and philosophical framework for using evidence, reason, and impartial welfare concern to identify where marginal resources can do the most good, especially through cause prioritization by scale, tractability, neglectedness, and counterfactual impact.

skills.name: Epicureanism
skills.description: Epicureanism is a Hellenistic ethical framework that seeks pleasure understood as tranquil freedom from pain and disturbance, using desire classification, prudent choice, friendship, naturalistic explanation, and the removal of death and divine fears.

skills.name: Epistemic Contextualism
skills.description: Epistemic Contextualism is the semantic view that the truth of knowledge attributions depends on the conversational context, so standards for saying someone 'knows' can shift with stakes, salience, and practical purposes.

skills.name: Error Statistics
skills.description: Error Statistics is Deborah Mayo's philosophy of statistical inference: evidence consists in claims passing severe tests, where a test is severe when it would probably have detected the specific error or discrepancy if it were present.

skills.name: Ethics of Belief | Clifford: wrong always and everywhere to believe on insufficient evidence
skills.description: Clifford's ethics of belief treats believing itself as morally assessable: because belief guides action, trains habits, and contributes to a shared social stock of trust, it is wrong always, everywhere, and for anyone to believe anything on insufficient evidence.

skills.name: Evidentialism
skills.description: Evidentialism is the Conee-Feldman view that epistemic justification is entirely determined by evidence: a belief is justified exactly when it fits the evidence possessed by the subject.

skills.name: Evolutionary Game Theory | ESS, replicator dynamics, strategies in populations
skills.description: Evolutionary Game Theory studies strategic behavior as competition among strategies in populations, using concepts such as evolutionarily stable strategies, replicator dynamics, invasion fitness, and frequency-dependent selection rather than assuming fully rational individual choice.

skills.name: Existential Risk Analysis
skills.description: Existential Risk Analysis evaluates hazards that could permanently destroy humanity's long-term potential through extinction, unrecoverable civilizational collapse, or irreversible value lock-in, using expected-value reasoning under extreme stakes and deep uncertainty.

skills.name: Expected Utility Theory
skills.description: Expected Utility Theory is the von Neumann-Morgenstern account of rational choice under risk: preferences satisfying specific axioms can be represented as maximizing the probability-weighted utility of outcomes.

skills.name: Expected-Value Reasoning
skills.description: Expected-Value Reasoning evaluates risky choices by multiplying each possible outcome's payoff by its probability and comparing the resulting weighted sums, with explicit attention to rare but high-impact tail outcomes.

skills.name: Experimental Philosophy | testing folk intuitions empirically to discipline philosophical claims
skills.description: Experimental Philosophy uses empirical methods, especially surveys and behavioral experiments, to test claims about folk intuitions and ordinary concepts that philosophers often rely on in arguments about knowledge, action, responsibility, meaning, and morality.

skills.name: Expressivism | moral claims express attitudes, not facts (Ayer, Blackburn, Gibbard)
skills.description: Expressivism explains moral language as expressing noncognitive attitudes, plans, endorsements, or prescriptions rather than describing independent moral facts, while later versions aim to preserve the logic and discipline of ordinary moral argument.

skills.name: Failure Mode and Effects Analysis
skills.description: Failure Mode and Effects Analysis is a preventive reliability method that decomposes a system, process, or design into functions, enumerates how each element can fail, scores the effects by severity, occurrence, and detection, and prioritizes controls or redesign before failure occurs.

skills.name: Fallacy Analysis
skills.description: Fallacy Analysis is the systematic identification and evaluation of fallacious patterns of reasoning — arguments that appear valid or persuasive but fail to establish their conclusions through logical defects, rhetorical manipulation, or unjustified premises. It operates as both a diagnostic tool (identifying where reasoning breaks down) and a normative standard (specifying what good argumentation requires).

skills.name: Falsificationism
skills.description: Falsificationism, developed by Karl Popper, holds that the demarcation criterion separating genuine science from non-science is falsifiability: a theory is scientific if and only if it makes predictions that could, in principle, be shown false by observation. Science advances not by confirming theories but by subjecting them to severe tests and eliminating those that fail.

skills.name: Fermi Estimation
skills.description: Fermi Estimation is an order-of-magnitude reasoning method that answers hard quantitative questions by decomposing an unknown into simpler factors, assigning rough but defensible values, and checking the resulting scale.

skills.name: First-Principles Reasoning
skills.description: First-Principles Reasoning decomposes a problem into fundamental truths, definitions, constraints, and causal mechanisms, then rebuilds conclusions or designs from those foundations instead of relying on analogy, convention, or inherited assumptions. It draws on Aristotelian first principles, Cartesian methodic doubt, and engineering practice.

skills.name: Foundationalism
skills.description: Foundationalism is an epistemological theory that answers the regress of justification by distinguishing basic beliefs, which possess non-inferential warrant, from nonbasic beliefs, whose justification depends on proper support from those foundations. It models knowledge as an epistemic structure with a grounded base and an inferential superstructure.

skills.name: Four Noble Truths
skills.description: The Four Noble Truths are the Buddha's first and foundational teaching, diagnosing the universal human condition of suffering (dukkha), identifying its origin in craving (tanha), declaring its cessation possible (nirodha), and prescribing the path to that cessation (magga). They constitute a clinical-soteriological framework: observe the symptom, trace its cause, confirm the cure exists, follow the treatment.

skills.name: Frequentist Hypothesis Testing
skills.description: Frequentist Hypothesis Testing is the family of null-hypothesis significance and error-control methods associated with Fisher, Neyman, and Pearson, using sampling distributions, p-values, rejection rules, and Type I/Type II error rates to test statistical hypotheses.

skills.name: Game Theory
skills.description: Game Theory is the mathematical study of strategic interaction among rational agents whose outcomes depend on the choices of all participants, providing tools to predict behavior, identify stable equilibria, and design incentive-compatible institutions. It covers both cooperative and non-cooperative games, zero-sum and variable-sum settings, and one-shot versus repeated interactions.

skills.name: General Semantics
skills.description: General Semantics is Korzybski's discipline for improving evaluation by distinguishing maps from territories, tracking levels of abstraction, and revising language habits that create identification, overgeneralization, and confused semantic reactions.

skills.name: Goodhart's Law | when a measure becomes a target it ceases to be a good measure
skills.description: Goodhart's Law explains how proxy measures degrade when used for control, incentives, or optimization, because agents adapt to the metric and the measured correlation with the real goal breaks down.

skills.name: Halakha
skills.description: Halakha is the collective body of Jewish religious law derived from the Written Torah, Oral Torah, rabbinic legislation, and communal custom, governing every domain of Jewish life from worship to commerce to interpersonal conduct. It functions as both a legal system and a spiritual discipline, framing righteous action as obligatory practice rather than optional virtue.

skills.name: Hayekian Spontaneous Order
skills.description: Hayekian Spontaneous Order explains how complex coordination can emerge from decentralized action under general rules, especially through price signals that communicate dispersed knowledge without central command.

skills.name: Hermeneutics | Gadamer/Schleiermacher: interpretation, hermeneutic circle, fusion of horizons
skills.description: Hermeneutics in the Schleiermacher-Gadamer tradition is the theory and practice of interpretation across linguistic, historical, and cultural distance, centered on the hermeneutic circle, historically effected understanding, prejudgment, and the fusion of horizons.

skills.name: Heuristics and Biases Program
skills.description: The Heuristics and Biases Program is Kahneman and Tversky's research program showing that human judgment under uncertainty often relies on simple heuristics that are efficient but produce systematic departures from probability theory, statistics, and rational choice norms.

skills.name: High-Reliability Organizations
skills.description: High-Reliability Organizations is Weick and Sutcliffe's framework for explaining how organizations operating hazardous, tightly coupled systems sustain very low failure rates through mindful organizing: preoccupation with failure, reluctance to simplify, sensitivity to operations, commitment to resilience, and deference to expertise.

skills.name: Humean Empiricism
skills.description: Humean Empiricism is David Hume's account of knowledge and belief as grounded in impressions, habit, and custom, sharply limiting reason's authority over causation, induction, selfhood, miracles, and practical motivation.

skills.name: Hypothetico-Deductive Method
skills.description: The Hypothetico-Deductive Method is a model of scientific inquiry in which investigators formulate hypotheses, deduce testable observational consequences from them with auxiliary assumptions, and evaluate the hypotheses by whether those consequences are borne out.

skills.name: Ignatian Discernment
skills.description: Ignatian Discernment is a structured spiritual practice developed by Ignatius of Loyola (1491–1556) in the Spiritual Exercises for making major decisions and detecting the movements of consolation and desolation in the soul, so as to align one's choices with God's will. It provides a practical methodology for navigating uncertainty, evaluating competing options, and distinguishing authentic spiritual promptings from self-deception or disordered attachment.

skills.name: Ijtihad
skills.description: Ijtihad is the Islamic jurisprudential principle of independent rational reasoning applied to derive legal rulings for situations not explicitly addressed by the Quran and Sunnah. It is the methodology by which qualified jurists (mujtahidun) exercise interpretive effort (the literal meaning of 'ijtihad' is exertion or striving) to extend, adapt, and reform Islamic law in response to new circumstances, enabling the Shariah to remain a living system rather than a fossilized code.

skills.name: Inference to the Best Explanation
skills.description: Inference to the Best Explanation (IBE), also called abduction, is the inferential practice of selecting the hypothesis that, if true, would provide the best explanation of the available evidence — where 'best' is assessed by explanatory virtues such as scope, simplicity, coherence, and fit. IBE is offered as a model of how scientists, diagnosticians, and ordinary reasoners actually justify belief.

skills.name: Information Asymmetry | Akerlof lemons, adverse selection, moral hazard
skills.description: Information Asymmetry analyzes markets and contracts where one party has private information about quality, risk, type, effort, or action, producing adverse selection before exchange and moral hazard after exchange.

skills.name: Instrumentalism | theories as prediction tools, not literal descriptions
skills.description: Instrumentalism treats scientific theories as tools for prediction, organization, and practical control rather than literal descriptions of observable and unobservable reality. It accepts theories for their empirical performance while withholding realist commitment to their theoretical posits.

skills.name: Inversion | solve problems backwards; avoid stupidity rather than seek brilliance (Jacobi, Munger)
skills.description: Inversion is a problem-solving heuristic that reverses the question, starting from failure, impossibility, or the desired end state to reveal constraints, hidden assumptions, and avoidable causes of bad outcomes.

skills.name: Jobs-to-be-Done
skills.description: Jobs-to-be-Done is Clayton Christensen's demand-side innovation framework: customers 'hire' products and services to accomplish progress in specific circumstances, so product strategy should be organized around the job rather than the buyer category or product category.

skills.name: Just War Theory
skills.description: Just War Theory is a tradition of moral reasoning — developed across Christian theology (Augustine, Aquinas), international law, and secular philosophy — that holds that war and organized violence are sometimes morally permissible but only under stringent conditions governing both the decision to go to war (jus ad bellum) and the conduct of war (jus in bello). It provides a systematic framework for evaluating the justice of armed conflict and coercive force.

skills.name: Kantian Deontology
skills.description: Kantian Deontology is Immanuel Kant's duty-based moral theory holding that the rightness of an action is determined by whether it conforms to universalizable moral laws derived from pure practical reason, not by its consequences. Rational agents have both the capacity and obligation to act from duty, treating humanity always as an end in itself and never merely as a means.

skills.name: Karma Yoga
skills.description: Karma Yoga is one of the four classical paths to liberation (moksha) in Hindu philosophy, systematically expounded in the Bhagavad Gita, centered on the discipline of selfless action—performing one's duty without attachment to results. It holds that right action, performed as an offering and without ego-investment in outcomes, purifies the mind and leads to spiritual liberation.

skills.name: Knowledge-First Epistemology
skills.description: Knowledge-First Epistemology, associated especially with Timothy Williamson, treats knowledge as a primitive, unanalyzable mental state that explains evidence, belief, assertion, and rational action rather than being explained by them.

skills.name: Kuhnian Paradigm Theory | normal science, anomaly, crisis, incommensurable paradigm shifts
skills.description: Kuhnian Paradigm Theory models science as alternating between normal puzzle-solving within a paradigm and revolutionary episodes in which accumulated anomalies and crisis lead to an incommensurable paradigm shift. It emphasizes exemplars, disciplinary matrices, anomaly management, and historically situated standards of theory choice.

skills.name: Lakatos's Research Programmes | progressive vs degenerating programmes; hard core + protective belt
skills.description: Lakatos's Methodology of Scientific Research Programmes evaluates science through historically extended programmes with a hard core, protective belt, negative heuristic, and positive heuristic. Programmes are judged by whether their problemshifts are progressive or degenerating relative to rivals.

skills.name: Leverage Points | Meadows: where to intervene in a system, ranked by power
skills.description: Leverage Points is Donella Meadows's ranked framework for finding places to intervene in a system, from shallow parameter tweaks to deep changes in information flows, rules, goals, and paradigms.

skills.name: Liberation Theology
skills.description: Liberation Theology is a theological movement that emerged primarily in Latin America in the 1960s–70s, holding that Christian faith requires a 'preferential option for the poor' and that authentic theology must be done from within the experience of the oppressed, integrating social analysis (often drawing on Marxist categories) with biblical hermeneutics and the prophetic tradition. It re-reads salvation history as including liberation from structural injustice as an integral dimension of redemption.

skills.name: Libertarianism | Nozick: self-ownership, side constraints, entitlement theory of justice
skills.description: Libertarianism | Nozick: self-ownership, side constraints, entitlement theory of justice treats persons as self-owning agents with rights that constrain what others and the state may do, grounding distributive justice in just acquisition, voluntary transfer, and rectification rather than patterned equality.

skills.name: Lindy Effect | expected longevity proportional to current age for non-perishables
skills.description: The Lindy Effect is a longevity heuristic for nonperishable things: the longer an idea, book, technology, institution, or practice has survived, the longer its expected remaining life, because age reveals survival through selection.

skills.name: Lojong
skills.description: Lojong (Tibetan: blo sbyong, 'mind training') is a tradition of Mahayana Buddhist contemplative practice originating in 11th-century Tibet with Atisha Dipamkara and systematized by Chekawa Yeshe Dorje in the Seven-Point Mind Training. It consists of aphoristic slogans (59 in Chekawa's system) that train the practitioner to transform adversity into the path, cultivate bodhicitta (the awakening mind), and dissolve self-cherishing through tonglen (taking and sending) meditation and moment-to-moment application.

skills.name: Luck Egalitarianism | neutralize unchosen luck, hold people to chosen risk
skills.description: Luck Egalitarianism | neutralize unchosen luck, hold people to chosen risk holds that distributive justice should compensate or neutralize disadvantages due to brute luck while allowing inequalities traceable to responsible choice or option luck.

skills.name: Maqasid al-Shariah
skills.description: Maqasid al-Shariah (the Objectives or Higher Purposes of Islamic Law) is a jurisprudential framework, systematized by al-Ghazali (1058–1111) and al-Shatibi (d. 1388), that identifies the overarching goals that Islamic law is designed to protect and promote — traditionally the five essentials: religion, life, intellect, lineage/family, and property. It enables jurists to reason about the spirit behind legal rulings, adjudicate novel cases, and evaluate whether a given rule or action serves or defeats Islamic law's ultimate purposes.

skills.name: Margin of Safety
skills.description: Margin of Safety is a decision and design principle, central in value investing and engineering, that requires a deliberate buffer between estimated capacity or value and the demanded load, price, or exposure so errors, uncertainty, and adverse variation do not produce ruin.

skills.name: Mechanism Design | reverse game theory: design rules so selfish play yields desired outcomes
skills.description: Mechanism Design is reverse game theory: it starts from a desired social outcome and designs rules, messages, allocations, and payments so that self-interested agents, acting on private information, produce that outcome in equilibrium.

skills.name: Minimax Regret
skills.description: Minimax Regret is a decision rule for uncertainty without reliable probabilities: compare each act by the largest regret it could produce relative to the best act in each state of the world, then choose the act whose worst regret is smallest.

skills.name: Minimum Description Length
skills.description: Minimum Description Length is an information-theoretic model selection principle, developed by Jorma Rissanen, that chooses the hypothesis or statistical model that permits the shortest lossless description of the data when both the model and its residual errors are encoded. It formalizes Occam's Razor by treating simplicity and fit as competing contributions to total codelength.

skills.name: Modal Reasoning
skills.description: Modal Reasoning is the formal and informal practice of reasoning about what is necessarily true (could not be otherwise), possibly true (could be true), and contingently true (happens to be true but could have been otherwise), using possible-worlds semantics and modal logic to clarify the logical status of claims and explore the space of alternatives. It underpins arguments in metaphysics, ethics, philosophy of mind, and any domain requiring distinctions between what is the case and what must or could be the case.

skills.name: Moral Realism | mind-independent moral facts; how to reason if they exist
skills.description: Moral Realism holds that some moral claims are truth-apt and true in virtue of moral facts that do not depend merely on anyone's attitudes, approvals, conventions, or prescriptions; moral reasoning aims to discover and respond to those facts.

skills.name: Moral Sentimentalism | Hume/Smith: moral judgments grounded in sentiment and sympathy
skills.description: Moral Sentimentalism explains moral judgment as grounded in human sentiments, especially sympathy with affected persons, rather than in pure reason alone: we approve traits and actions through regulated feelings shaped by common human nature and a stable point of view.

skills.name: Mussar
skills.description: Mussar is a Jewish ethical and spiritual discipline originating in 19th-century Lithuania with Rabbi Israel Salanter, focused on the deliberate cultivation of moral character traits (middot) through structured introspection, study, and practice. It treats character development as a rigorous, ongoing discipline comparable to Talmud study—not a passive aspiration but an active daily regimen.

skills.name: Naturalized Epistemology
skills.description: Naturalized Epistemology, in Quine's programmatic sense, reconceives epistemology as part of empirical psychology: the scientific study of how sensory input and cognitive mechanisms generate theories of the world, without appeal to an external first philosophy.

skills.name: Network Effects and Lock-in
skills.description: Network Effects and Lock-in analyzes markets where value rises with adoption, complements, and compatibility, producing tipping, path dependence, switching costs, platform strategy, and durable dominance.

skills.name: Normal Accidents Theory | Perrow: tight coupling + interactive complexity make accidents inevitable
skills.description: Normal Accidents Theory is Charles Perrow's framework for explaining why accidents become inevitable in systems that combine tight coupling with interactive complexity, especially high-risk technologies such as nuclear power, chemical plants, aircraft, and other tightly integrated socio-technical systems.

skills.name: Nudge and Choice Architecture
skills.description: Nudge and Choice Architecture designs the context in which decisions are made so predictable cognitive biases, defaults, framing effects, and salience steer behavior while preserving freedom of choice.

skills.name: Occam's Razor
skills.description: Occam's Razor is the methodological principle that, when competing explanations account for the same evidence equally well, the simpler one — the one that posits fewer entities, assumptions, or mechanisms — is to be preferred. It functions as a tie-breaking heuristic in theory choice, not as a metaphysical guarantee that reality is simple.

skills.name: OODA Loop
skills.description: The OODA Loop is John Boyd's tempo-based framework for competition and conflict: actors observe, orient, decide, and act while trying to adapt faster and more coherently than opponents. Its center of gravity is orientation, where information, culture, experience, analysis, synthesis, and unfolding action shape perception and choice.

skills.name: Opportunity Cost and Marginal Analysis | think at the margin; cost as foregone alternative
skills.description: Opportunity Cost and Marginal Analysis evaluates choices by comparing the value of the next-best foregone alternative with the incremental benefits and costs of one more unit of action, correcting decisions that focus on visible spending, sunk costs, or totals instead of margins.

skills.name: Ordinary Language Philosophy
skills.description: Ordinary Language Philosophy holds that philosophical problems are typically generated by the misuse or distortion of ordinary language — by taking words out of their everyday contexts and expecting them to perform functions they were not designed for. Philosophical therapy consists in returning to ordinary usage to dissolve, rather than solve, philosophical pseudo-problems.

skills.name: Ostrom's Commons Governance
skills.description: Ostrom's Commons Governance explains how communities can sustainably manage common-pool resources through locally crafted rules, monitoring, graduated sanctions, conflict-resolution mechanisms, and nested institutions.

skills.name: Overton Window
skills.description: The Overton Window is a political strategy framework describing the range of policy ideas that are publicly acceptable enough for officials to support, and how advocacy, events, and institutions can shift that range over time.

skills.name: Pareto Principle | 80/20 concentration of effects; vital few vs trivial many
skills.description: The Pareto Principle is a concentration heuristic for analyzing skewed outcome distributions: in many domains, a small minority of inputs, causes, customers, products, or defects produces a large majority of effects. It directs attention to the vital few before the trivial many.

skills.name: Pascal's Wager
skills.description: Pascal's Wager is a decision-theoretic argument by Blaise Pascal (Pensées, c. 1660) that it is rationally required to wager on the existence of God, because if God exists the expected utility of belief is infinite while the cost of belief is finite, making belief the dominant strategy regardless of the prior probability of God's existence.

skills.name: Path Dependence | history-contingent outcomes, QWERTY effects, critical junctures
skills.description: Path Dependence explains history-contingent outcomes in which early events, critical junctures, increasing returns, switching costs, and institutional or technological lock-in shape later possibilities and make some trajectories hard to reverse.

skills.name: Pikuach Nefesh
skills.description: Pikuach Nefesh (Hebrew: 'watching over a soul') is the halakhic principle that the preservation of human life overrides nearly all other Jewish religious commandments, including Shabbat, Yom Kippur, and most dietary laws. It establishes a clear priority hierarchy: when life is at risk, the obligation to save it takes precedence over virtually every other religious duty.

skills.name: Pragmatism
skills.description: Pragmatism is a philosophical tradition originating with Charles Sanders Peirce, William James, and John Dewey that evaluates beliefs, theories, and meanings by their practical consequences and problem-solving utility. It rejects the idea that truth is a correspondence to mind-independent reality, instead treating truth as what works — what proves fruitful, coherent, and useful in guiding inquiry and action.

skills.name: Pre-mortem
skills.description: Pre-mortem is Gary Klein's prospective-hindsight technique: before committing to a plan, assume it has failed disastrously and ask the team to explain why so latent risks can be surfaced and mitigated.

skills.name: Principal-Agent Analysis
skills.description: Principal-Agent Analysis examines delegation under incentive conflict and information asymmetry, explaining how principals can design contracts, monitoring, governance, and accountability mechanisms to reduce agency costs.

skills.name: Principle of Charity
skills.description: The Principle of Charity is a methodological norm in argument interpretation and philosophical analysis holding that one should reconstruct an argument or position in its strongest, most reasonable form before criticizing it. First systematized in philosophy of language and interpretation theory, it ensures that disagreements are genuine and productive rather than targeting distorted versions of opposing views.

skills.name: Prospect Theory
skills.description: Prospect Theory is Kahneman and Tversky's descriptive model of risky choice, centered on reference dependence, loss aversion, diminishing sensitivity, and nonlinear probability weighting.

skills.name: Public Choice Theory | politics as exchange among self-interested actors; rent-seeking
skills.description: Public Choice Theory applies economic reasoning to politics, treating voters, politicians, bureaucrats, regulators, and interest groups as incentive-responsive actors engaged in political exchange, collective choice, and rent seeking.

skills.name: Queueing Theory Thinking | utilization vs latency, variability, Little's law intuitions
skills.description: Queueing Theory Thinking applies queueing intuitions to service, operations, software, support, healthcare, networks, and project flow: utilization, variability, arrival rates, service rates, queue discipline, and Little's law explain why small capacity margins can produce large delays.

skills.name: Rationalism | Descartes/Spinoza/Leibniz: knowledge from reason, innate ideas, method of doubt
skills.description: Rationalism, in the early modern tradition of Descartes, Spinoza, and Leibniz, treats reason as an independent source of knowledge, emphasizes innate ideas or principles, and uses methodic doubt and deductive system-building to seek foundations more secure than sensory experience.

skills.name: Red Teaming
skills.description: Red Teaming is an institutionalized adversarial practice in which an independent group attacks a plan, system, or belief from the perspective of an opponent or failure agent to expose vulnerabilities before commitment.

skills.name: Reflective Equilibrium
skills.description: Reflective Equilibrium is a method of moral justification developed by John Rawls in which we achieve coherence between our considered moral judgments (intuitions about cases) and general moral principles by iteratively revising both until they cohere. It treats neither pure theory nor raw intuition as foundational, but uses each to check and refine the other.

skills.name: Reliabilism
skills.description: Reliabilism is an externalist theory of knowledge and justification, associated especially with Alvin Goldman, according to which a belief is epistemically justified or qualifies as knowledge when it is formed by cognitive processes that reliably produce true beliefs in the relevant circumstances. It shifts attention from accessible reasons to the truth-conducive performance of belief-forming mechanisms.

skills.name: Replication and Robustness Thinking
skills.description: Replication and Robustness Thinking evaluates whether empirical claims hold across independent repeats, datasets, methods, analytic specifications, laboratories, and populations, treating single findings as fragile until their stability and boundary conditions are known.

skills.name: Resilience Engineering | safety as adaptive capacity, work-as-done vs work-as-imagined
skills.description: Resilience Engineering is a safety framework that treats safety as adaptive capacity in complex socio-technical systems, emphasizing work-as-done versus work-as-imagined, performance variability, drift, and the capacities to monitor, respond, learn, and anticipate.

skills.name: Robust Decision Making
skills.description: Robust Decision Making is RAND's analytic framework for choosing strategies that perform satisfactorily across many plausible futures by stress-testing options, identifying vulnerabilities, and adaptively improving plans under deep uncertainty.

skills.name: Root Cause Analysis
skills.description: Root Cause Analysis is a post-incident investigation framework for tracing an adverse event from immediate symptoms through causal chains to correctable physical, human, process, and systemic causes, using tools such as 5 Whys, fishbone diagrams, timelines, and corrective-action verification.

skills.name: Rule Consequentialism
skills.description: Rule Consequentialism is the consequentialist framework that evaluates moral rules, codes, or principles by their consequences under general acceptance, and then evaluates individual actions by whether they comply with the best justified rules. It preserves consequentialist grounding while explaining why promise-keeping, rights, and prohibitions on killing or punishing the innocent can bind in particular cases.

skills.name: Satisficing
skills.description: Satisficing is Herbert Simon's decision rule of selecting the first available alternative that meets an aspiration level, replacing exhaustive optimization when search, information, and computation are limited.

skills.name: Scientific Realism | unobservables posited by mature science approximately exist; no-miracles argument
skills.description: Scientific Realism holds that mature, predictively successful science gives approximately true descriptions of both observable and unobservable aspects of the world. Its signature no-miracles argument claims that scientific success would be inexplicable if central theoretical posits did not approximately exist.

skills.name: Second-Order Thinking | and-then-what: downstream and reflexive consequences
skills.description: Second-Order Thinking is the discipline of asking 'and then what?' before acting: it evaluates downstream, indirect, delayed, incentive, and reflexive consequences rather than stopping at immediate effects.

skills.name: Signaling Theory | costly signals as credibility (Spence, Zahavi handicap principle)
skills.description: Signaling Theory explains how informed actors credibly communicate hidden quality, type, intention, or fitness through observable signals whose costs make imitation unattractive for lower-quality senders.

skills.name: Social Contract Theory
skills.description: Social Contract Theory explains political legitimacy as arising from consent, authorization, or collective agreement among persons who establish common authority. In Locke and Rousseau, the framework asks what people would or did authorize in moving from natural freedom to civil society, and when government remains faithful to that authorizing purpose.

skills.name: Socratic Method | elenchus
skills.description: Socratic Method | elenchus is definition-seeking cross-examination that elicits a person's commitments, tests them for contradiction, and uses the resulting aporia to clarify what is and is not yet known.

skills.name: Speech Act Theory | Austin/Searle: locutionary/illocutionary/perlocutionary acts; performatives
skills.description: Speech Act Theory analyzes language as action, distinguishing what is said, what act is performed in saying it, and what effects are produced by saying it, with special attention to performatives, felicity conditions, illocutionary force, and institutional context.

skills.name: Steelmanning
skills.description: Steelmanning is the discipline of reconstructing the strongest defensible version of an opposing view before evaluating or rebutting it, replacing caricature with charitable, adversarially robust understanding.

skills.name: Stoicism | dichotomy of control, virtue as the only good, negative visualization
skills.description: Stoicism is a Hellenistic virtue ethics and practical discipline that locates human flourishing in rational virtue, distinguishes what is up to us from what is not, and trains judgment so external events do not rule the soul.

skills.name: Strong Inference
skills.description: Strong Inference is John R. Platt's scientific method of formulating multiple alternative hypotheses, devising crucial experiments that exclude some of them, and iterating the process as a branching program of inquiry.

skills.name: Sufism
skills.description: Sufism (tasawwuf) is the inner, mystical, and ascetic dimension of Islam, focused on the purification of the soul (tazkiyat al-nafs), direct experiential knowledge of God (marifah), and the journey of the heart toward annihilation of the ego-self in the divine (fana) and subsistence in God (baqa). It provides a framework for diagnosing the spiritual conditions of the soul, prescribing spiritual disciplines, and interpreting experience through the lens of the lover-Beloved relationship between the soul and God.

skills.name: Systems Thinking
skills.description: Systems Thinking analyzes problems as behavior generated by interacting stocks, flows, feedback loops, delays, boundaries, and leverage points rather than as linear chains of isolated causes. In the Meadows and Forrester tradition, it uses system structure to explain recurring patterns such as growth, overshoot, oscillation, resilience, and policy resistance.

skills.name: Tawhid
skills.description: Tawhid is the central theological doctrine of Islam — the absolute unity and uniqueness of God (Allah) — which functions not only as a creedal affirmation but as a comprehensive framework for organizing all of reality, ethics, knowledge, and social life. As a framework, Tawhid demands that every dimension of existence be oriented toward, and understood in relation to, the one undivided divine reality, making it the ontological and ethical foundation from which all Islamic thought and action derive.

skills.name: Temporal Discounting
skills.description: Temporal Discounting analyzes how agents value costs and benefits that occur at different times, with special attention to present bias, hyperbolic discounting, preference reversal, and intertemporal self-control.

skills.name: The Four Purusharthas
skills.description: The Four Purusharthas (Sanskrit: purusha-artha, 'human aims') is the classical Hindu framework identifying four irreducible goals of human life—Dharma (right conduct), Artha (prosperity and power), Kama (pleasure and desire), and Moksha (liberation)—held to encompass all legitimate human striving and to require integration rather than exclusive pursuit of any single aim.

skills.name: The Gettier Problem
skills.description: The Gettier Problem, introduced by Edmund Gettier in a landmark 1963 paper, is the demonstration that the classical tripartite analysis of knowledge as justified true belief is insufficient: a subject can have a justified true belief that nonetheless does not constitute knowledge, because the belief's truth is accidental relative to the justification. It is both a specific philosophical problem and a general methodological lesson about how to test theories by counterexample.

skills.name: The Is-Ought Distinction
skills.description: The Is-Ought Distinction, identified by David Hume in the Treatise of Human Nature (1739), is the logical observation that normative conclusions ('ought' claims) cannot be validly derived from purely descriptive premises ('is' claims) alone. Any argument that moves from facts about the world to moral obligations requires at least one normative premise, and the failure to acknowledge this gap is a persistent error in moral reasoning.

skills.name: The Map-Territory Distinction
skills.description: The Map-Territory Distinction is the epistemological principle, originating with Alfred Korzybski's dictum 'the map is not the territory,' that any model, concept, theory, or representation of reality is necessarily an abstraction that omits, distorts, or simplifies the territory it depicts — and that confusing the map with the territory generates systematic errors in reasoning and action. The distinction is foundational to critical thinking about models, language, and cognition.

skills.name: The Middle Way
skills.description: The Middle Way (Pali: majjhima patipada; Sanskrit: madhyama pratipad) is the Buddha's principle of avoiding extremes — originally the avoidance of sensual indulgence and self-mortification as paths to liberation, later generalized into a broad epistemological and ethical orientation that seeks the mean between paired opposites: existence and non-existence, eternalism and nihilism, rigid rule-following and moral laxity. In Madhyamaka philosophy, Nagarjuna extended it to the metaphysical: the middle between 'things exist absolutely' and 'things do not exist at all.'

skills.name: The Noble Eightfold Path
skills.description: The Noble Eightfold Path (Ariya Atthangika Magga) is the Buddha's fourth Noble Truth made explicit: an eight-factor program of mental, ethical, and meditative cultivation that constitutes the path to the cessation of suffering. The eight factors — spanning wisdom, ethical conduct, and mental development — are to be cultivated simultaneously rather than sequentially, as mutually reinforcing aspects of a unified practice.

skills.name: The Ship of Theseus
skills.description: The Ship of Theseus is a classical philosophical puzzle about personal identity and persistence through change: if an object (or person, institution, or concept) is gradually replaced piece by piece until none of the original material remains, is it still the same object? The puzzle probes competing criteria of identity — material continuity, functional continuity, and psychological/historical continuity — and reveals that our everyday identity concepts can be indeterminate or context-dependent.

skills.name: Theory of Constraints
skills.description: Theory of Constraints is Eliyahu M. Goldratt's management framework for improving a goal-directed system by identifying the binding constraint, exploiting it, subordinating other work to it, elevating it if needed, and repeating as the constraint moves. It emphasizes throughput, inventory, operating expense, and global rather than local optimization.

skills.name: Thomistic Natural Law
skills.description: Thomistic Natural Law is the moral and political philosophy developed by Thomas Aquinas (1225–1274) holding that human reason can discern objective moral norms grounded in human nature and ultimately in God's Eternal Law. It provides a framework for evaluating actions, laws, and institutions by asking whether they fulfill or frustrate the natural ends (teloi) of human beings.

skills.name: Thought Experiments
skills.description: Thought Experiments are deliberately constructed hypothetical scenarios used to isolate and test the logical consequences of concepts, principles, or theories by reasoning through what would follow in an imagined situation, without the need for physical execution. They function as a primary instrument of philosophical argument, scientific theorizing, and ethical analysis by pumping intuitions, revealing hidden commitments, or demonstrating impossibilities.

skills.name: Tikkun Olam
skills.description: Tikkun Olam (Hebrew: 'repair of the world') is a Jewish concept holding that human beings are partners with the divine in the ongoing work of repairing, completing, and perfecting the world through ethical action, justice, and compassion. In its modern form, it functions as a framework for social justice and moral responsibility oriented toward systemic betterment of the world.

skills.name: Tragedy of the Commons | Hardin: individually rational overuse of shared resources
skills.description: Tragedy of the Commons is Garrett Hardin's framework for cases where individually rational use of an open-access shared resource produces collective overuse, depletion, or pollution because private benefits are concentrated while costs are shared.

skills.name: TRIZ
skills.description: TRIZ is Genrich Altshuller's theory of inventive problem solving, using contradiction analysis, ideality, resources, and recurring inventive principles to generate non-compromise technical solutions.

skills.name: Utilitarianism
skills.description: Utilitarianism is a consequentialist moral theory holding that the right action is the one that produces the greatest overall well-being (utility) for all affected parties. Associated principally with Jeremy Bentham and John Stuart Mill, it evaluates acts and policies solely by their outcomes.

skills.name: Veil of Ignorance
skills.description: The Veil of Ignorance is a thought experiment devised by John Rawls in which principles of justice are chosen by rational agents who do not know their own place in society — their class, sex, race, natural talents, or conception of the good. By eliminating self-interested bias, the device generates principles that are fair to all because they are chosen by none who knows their own advantage.

skills.name: Verificationism
skills.description: Verificationism is the semantic and epistemological thesis, associated with the Logical Positivists of the Vienna Circle, that the meaning of a statement consists in the conditions under which it is empirically verifiable; statements that are neither empirically verifiable nor analytically true are held to be cognitively meaningless. It was proposed as a demarcation criterion to eliminate metaphysics, theology, and pseudo-scientific claims from legitimate discourse.

skills.name: Virtue Epistemology
skills.description: Virtue Epistemology evaluates knowledge, justification, and inquiry through intellectual virtues and vices: stable excellences or competences of the knower that make true belief creditable to the agent rather than merely lucky.

skills.name: Wu Wei
skills.description: Wu Wei (無為, 'non-action' or 'effortless action') is the central operative concept of Daoist philosophy, originating in the Daodejing attributed to Laozi and developed in the Zhuangzi. It names a mode of acting in accordance with the natural flow of the Dao — not forced, contrived, or ego-driven action, but spontaneous, responsive conduct that achieves its ends without struggle, resistance, or unnecessary assertion. Wu wei is not passivity but the highest form of efficacy: acting so naturally and appropriately that action appears effortless.
